item.title: Anthropic’s Claude 3.7 Sonnet hybrid reasoning model is now available in Amazon Bedrock
item.author: Esra Kayabali
item.content:encoded:
Amazon Bedrock is expanding its foundation model (FM) offerings as the generative AI field evolves. Today, we’re excited to announce the availability of Anthropic’s Claude 3.7 Sonnet foundation model in Amazon Bedrock. As Anthropic’s most intelligent model to date, Claude 3.7 Sonnet stands out as their first hybrid reasoning model capable of producing quick responses or , meaning it can work through difficult problems using careful, step-by-step reasoning. Additionally, today we are adding Claude 3.7 Sonnet to the list of models used by Amazon Q Developer. Amazon Q is built on Bedrock, and with Amazon Q you can use the most appropriate model for a specific task such as Claude 3.7 Sonnet, for more advanced coding workflows that enable developers to accelerate building across the entire software development lifecycle. Here are several notable features and capabilities of Claude 3.7 Sonnet in Amazon Bedrock.The first Claude model with hybrid reasoning – Claude 3.7 Sonnet takes a different approach to how models think. Instead of using separate models—one for quick answers and another for solving complex problems—Claude 3.7 Sonnet integrates reasoning as a core capability within a single model. This combination is more similar to how the human brains works. After all, we use the same brain whether we’re answering a simple question or solving a difficult puzzle.The model has two modes— and mode—which can be toggled in Amazon Bedrock. In mode, Claude 3.7 Sonnet is an improved version of Claude 3.5 Sonnet. In mode, Claude 3.7 Sonnet takes additional time to analyze problems in detail, plan solutions, and consider multiple perspectives before providing a response, allowing it to make further gains in performance. You can control speed and cost by choosing when to use reasoning capabilities. Extended thinking tokens count towards the context window and are billed as output tokens.Anthropic’s most powerful model for coding – Claude 3.7 Sonnet is state-of-the art for coding, excelling in understanding context and creative problem solving, and according to Anthropic, achieves an industry-leading 70.3% for mode on SWE-bench Verified. Claude 3.7 Sonnet also performs better than Claude 3.5 Sonnet across the majority of benchmarks. These enhanced capabilities make Claude 3.7 Sonnet ideal for powering AI agents and complex workflows.Over 15x longer output capacity than its predecessor – Compared to Claude 3.5 Sonnet, this model offers significantly expanded output length. This enhanced capacity is particularly useful when you explicitly request more detail, ask for multiple examples, or request additional context or background information. To achieve long outputs, try asking for a detailed outline (for writing use cases, you can specify outline detail down to the paragraph level and include word count targets). Then, ask for the response to index its paragraphs to the outline and reiterate the word counts. Claude 3.7 Sonnet supports outputs up to 128K tokens long (up to 64K as generally available and up to 128K as a beta).Adjustable reasoning budget – You can control the budget for thinking when you use Claude 3.7 Sonnet in Amazon Bedrock. This flexibility helps you weigh the trade-offs between speed, cost, and performance. By allocating more tokens to reasoning for complex problems or limiting tokens for faster responses, you can optimize performance for your specific use case. As for any new model, I have to request access in the Amazon Bedrock console. In the navigation pane, I choose under Bedrock configurations. Then, I choose to request access for Claude 3.7 Sonnet.To try Claude 3.7 Sonnet, I choose under in the navigation pane. Then I choose and choose under the and under the . To enable the mode, I toggle under . I type the following prompt, and choose :You're the manager of a small restaurant facing these challenges:
Three staff members called in sick for tonight's dinner service You're expecting a full house (80 seats) There's a large party of 20 coming at 7 PM Your main chef is available but two kitchen helpers are among those who called in sick You have 2 regular servers and 1 trainee available How would you:
Reorganize the available staff to handle the situation Prioritize tasks and service Determine if you need to make any adjustments to reservations Handle the large party while maintaining service quality Minimize negative impact on customer experience Explain your reasoning for each decision and discuss potential trade-offsHere’s the result with an animated image showing the reasoning process of the model.To test image-to-text vision capabilities, I upload an image of a detailed architectural site plan created using Amazon Bedrock. I receive a detailed analysis and reasoned insights of this site plan. Claude 3.7 Sonnet’s enhanced capabilities can benefit multiple industry use cases. Businesses can create advanced AI assistants and agents that interact directly with customers. In fields such as healthcare, it can assist in medical imaging analysis and research summarization, and financial services can benefit from its abilities to solve complex financial modeling problems. For developers, it serves as a coding companion that can review code, explain technical concepts, and suggest improvements across different languages.Anthropic’s Claude 3.7 Sonnet is available today in the US East (N. Virginia), US East (Ohio), and US West (Oregon) Regions. Check the full Region list for future updates.Claude 3.7 Sonnet is priced competitively and matches the price of Claude 3.5 Sonnet. For pricing details, refer to the Amazon Bedrock pricing page.— Esra2/24/2025: Base models screenshot updated.

item.title: AWS Weekly Roundup: Cloud Club Captain Applications, Formula 1®, Amazon Nova Prompt Engineering, and more (Feb 24, 2025)
item.author: Elizabeth Fuentes
item.content:encoded: AWS Developer Day 2025, held on February 20th, showcased how to integrate responsible generative AI into development workflows. The event featured keynotes from AWS leaders including Srini Iragavarapu, Director Generative AI Applications and Developer Experiences, Jeff Barr, Vice President of AWS Evangelism, David Nalley, Director Open Source Marketing of AWS, along with AWS Heroes and technical community members. Watch the full event recording on Developer Day 2025. Here are some launches that got my attention: AWS now enables you to set up backup payment methods that automatically activate if primary payment fails. This helps prevent service interruptions and reduces manual intervention for invoice payments. Here are additional noteworthy items: Here are my personal favorites posts from community.aws:Check your calendars and sign up for these upcoming AWS events: – Join a free online conference focusing on generative AI and data innovations. Available in multiple geographic regions: APJC and EMEA (March 6), North America (March 13), Greater China Region (March 14), and Latin America (April 8). – Join free online and in-person events that bring the cloud computing community together to connect, collaborate, and learn about AWS. Register in your nearest city: Paris (April 9), Amsterdam (April 16), London (April 30), and Poland (May 5). – AWS re:Inforce (June 16–18) in Philadelphia, PA our annual learning event devoted to all things AWS cloud security. Registration opens in March, and be ready to join more than 5,000 security builders and leaders.Create your AWS Builder ID and reserve your alias. Builder ID is a universal login credential that gives you access–beyond the AWS Management Console–to AWS tools and resources, including over 600 free training courses, community features, and developer tools such as Amazon Q Developer.That’s all for this week. Stay tuned for next week’s Weekly Roundup! series. Check back each week for a quick roundup of interesting news and announcements from AWS!

item.title: How to Automatically Restart Docker Containers After Reboots (EC2 & Linux Servers)
item.author: Jaecom
item.content:encoded: Learn how to automate Docker container restarts on AWS EC2 or Linux servers using systemd. No more manual reboots — get your containers up in seconds!Manually restarting Docker containers after every EC2 reboot can be frustrating. By using systemd, you can automatically run a custom script that brings containers back up each time the server restarts. This guide will show you how to:Create a shell script to manage container restarts.Configure a systemd service to execute the script.Enable and test the service so it runs on every reboot.start-containers.sh ScriptFirst, you’ll need a shell script that stops and removes old containers, then spins up fresh Docker containers every time it’s run. This script will later be used by our service to handle container restarts on every reboot.Create a directory for the script:cd ~mkdir reboot-scriptvim reboot-script/start-containers.shCreate and edit the script to stop, remove & start your Docker containers:#!/bin/bash# Stop and remove old containers if they existdocker container stop nginx-container campable-server || truedocker container rm nginx-container campable-server || truedocker run -p 8000:8000 -d --name campable-server --network campable-network campable-server# Using Docker Compose for my Nginx containerdocker compose -f /home/ubuntu/nginx/nginx-compose.yml up -ddocker-reboot-containers.service FileA systemdservice tells the operating system exactly how and when to run a process. Here, it will execute the start-containers.sh script after Docker is ready.Create the service file with root privileges:sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/docker-reboot-containers.serviceAdd the following configuration:[Unit]Description=Docker Container RestartAfter=docker.service# The file path of your start-containers.shExecStart=/bin/bash /home/ubuntu/reboot-script/start-containers.sh# Automatically restart if service failsRestart=on-failure# How long to wait before attempting another restartRestartSec=10# Limit the number of restarts in a given intervalStartLimitBurst=5WantedBy=multi-user.targetRequires=docker.service ensures Docker is running before the script starts.Restart=on-failure tells systemd service to restart only when the process exits with a non-zero exit code, which typically indicates a failure.StartLimitBurst=5 and StartLimitIntervalSec=70 prevent excessive restart attempts by exiting the restart if the number of restarts exceeds 5 times in 70 seconds.Step 3: Enable and Start the Systemd ServiceNow that the service file is ready, we need to have systemd recognize the service and enable it to run every time the system boots.The service file is located at /etc/systemd/system/docker-reboot-containers.service. When enabling the service, automatically searches for /etc/systemd/system directory (and other standard directories, such as /lib/systemd/system). This is important because it ensures that your service file is properly registered and linked within the Enable the docker-reboot-containers service:# Reload systemd configsudo systemctl daemon-reloadsudo systemctl enable docker-reboot-containersYou should see output like this:Created symlink /etc/systemd/system/multi-user.target.wants/docker-reboot-containers.service → /etc/systemd/system/docker-reboot-containers.service.This confirms that systemd has created a symlink in the multi-user.target.wants directory, pointing to your service file, and the service is now set to run on system boot.sudo systemctl start docker-reboot-containers● docker-reboot-containers.service - Docker Container Restart Loaded: loaded (/etc/systemd/system/docker-reboot-containers.service; enabled; preset: enabled) Active: active (running) since Sun 2025-01-12 12:58:53 UTC; 8s ago Main PID: 26123 (bash) Memory: 7.2M (peak: 7.5M) CGroup: /system.slice/docker-reboot-containers.service ├─26123 /bin/bash /home/ubuntu/reboot-script/start-containers.sh └─26130 docker container stop nginx-container web-serverIt seems to have successfully executed the script! To double check, list the the docker processes to see if the containers have been deleted and recreated:sudo docker ps# Created 6 seconds ago, the service is working!CONTAINER ID IMAGE COMMAND CREATED STATUS PORTS NAMES680717d82eea nginx:latest "/docker-entrypoint.…" 6 seconds ago Up 5 seconds 0.0.0.0:80->80/tcp, :::80->80/tcp, 0.0.0.0:443->443/tcp, :::443->443/tcp nginx-container14ed6d48b07b web-server "gunicorn --bind 0.0…" 6 seconds ago Up 6 seconds 0.0.0.0:8000->8000/tcp, :::8000->8000/tcp web-serverThe docker containers were created 6 seconds ago, confirming that our docker-reboot-containers service has successfully run.Step 4: Confirm at RebootNow, let’s reboot our EC2 instance and check if the service executes our script automatically.Perform a reboot of your EC2 Container:SSH into your EC Container, and check the Docker processes again:sudo docker ps# Created 32 seconds ago, the service is working!CONTAINER ID IMAGE COMMAND CREATED STATUS PORTS NAMES680717d82eea nginx:latest "/docker-entrypoint.…" 32 seconds ago Up 30 seconds 0.0.0.0:80->80/tcp, :::80->80/tcp, 0.0.0.0:443->443/tcp, :::443->443/tcp nginx-container14ed6d48b07b web-server "gunicorn --bind 0.0…" 32 seconds ago Up 30 seconds 0.0.0.0:8000->8000/tcp, :::8000->8000/tcp web-serverWith the Docker containers created 32 seconds ago, the service seems to be successfully working.By creating a simple script and tying it to a systemd service, we can eliminate the need to manually restart our Docker containers on every reboot. This approach saves time, reduces human error, and ensures our services are always up and running quickly. Happy automating!

item.title: Kubernetes the Hard Way on Raspberry Pi , Docker & EC2 (Part 4): Control Plane, Worker Nodes &…
item.author: Jaecom
item.content:encoded: Kubernetes the Hard Way on Raspberry Pi , Docker & EC2 (Part 4): Control Plane, Worker Nodes & TestingWelcome to , the final installment of our Kubernetes the Hard Way series on ! At this point, we have:A via WireGuard VPN and for Kubernetes componentsA key-value storeNow, we’ll bring it all together by:Deploying the Bringing worker nodes fully onlineConfiguring pod networkingRunning a (including a sample Nginx deployment)1. Bootstrapping the Kubernetes Control PlaneThe control plane consists of the , , and . We’ll copy the necessary binaries and systemd unit files to the server container, configure them, and then start the services.On your jumpbox container, copy the Kubernetes binaries and systemd unit files to the server container:scp \ downloads-amd/kube-apiserver \ downloads-amd/kube-controller-manager \ downloads-amd/kube-scheduler \ units/kube-apiserver.service \ units/kube-controller-manager.service \ units/kube-scheduler.service \ configs/kube-scheduler.yaml \ configs/kube-apiserver-to-kubelet.yaml \1.2 Install kubernetes controller binariesmkdir -p /etc/kubernetes/config chmod +x kube-apiserver \ kube-controller-manager \ kube-scheduler kubectl kube-controller-manager \ /usr/local/bin/1.3 Configure the following: kube-apiserver, kube-controller-manager, kube-scheduler:mkdir -p /var/lib/kubernetes/ kube-api-server.key kube-api-server.crt \ service-accounts.key service-accounts.crt \ encryption-config.yaml \Move the systemd unit files and configurations:mv kube-apiserver.service \ /etc/systemd/system/kube-apiserver.servicemv kube-controller-manager.kubeconfig /var/lib/kubernetes/mv kube-controller-manager.service /etc/systemd/system/mv kube-scheduler.kubeconfig /var/lib/kubernetes/mv kube-scheduler.yaml /etc/kubernetes/config/mv kube-scheduler.service /etc/systemd/system/1.4 Start the Controller Servicessystemctl daemon-reloadsystemctl enable kube-apiserver \ kube-controller-manager kube-schedulersystemctl start kube-apiserver \ kube-controller-manager kube-schedulerkubectl cluster-info \ --kubeconfig admin.kubeconfigYou should see something like:Kubernetes control plane is running at https://127.0.0.1:6443Check the status for any errors:systemctl status kube-apiserver kube-controller-manager kube-schedulerYou might see errors related to cluster role permissions; we’ll fix those next.1.5 RBAC for Kubelet AuthorizationCreate the system:kube-apiserver-to-kubelet ClusterRole with permissions to access the Kubelet API:kubectl apply -f kube-apiserver-to-kubelet.yaml \ --kubeconfig admin.kubeconfigRestart the controller services to apply the role permissions: systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart kube-apiserver \ kube-controller-manager kube-schedulerCheck the staus of kube-apiserver for any errors:systemctl status kube-apiserver kube-controller-manager kube-scheduler1.6 API Server VerificationFrom the jumpbox, verify the API server is reachable:curl -k --cacert ca.crt https://server.kubernetes.local:6443/versionYou should see JSON output with version details:{ "major": "1", "gitVersion": "v1.32.0", "gitCommit": "70d3cc986aa8221cd1dfb1121852688902d3bf53", "gitTreeState": "clean", "buildDate": "2024-12-11T17:59:15Z", "goVersion": "go1.23.3", "platform": "linux/amd64"2. Bootstrapping the Kubernetes Worker NodesNext, we’ll install the , , and on each worker node: node-0 (EC2 B) and node-1(Raspberry Pi).From the jumpbox, copy the correct binaries and configs to each worker node:for host in node-0 node-1; do SUBNET=$(grep $host machines.txt | cut -d " " -f 4) sed "s|SUBNET|$SUBNET|g" \ configs/10-bridge.conf > 10-bridge.conf sed "s|SUBNET|$SUBNET|g" \ configs/kubelet-config.yaml > kubelet-config.yaml scp 10-bridge.conf kubelet-config.yaml \ root@$host:~/Then copy the rest of the binaries and config files appropriately to node-0 (AMD64) and node-1 (ARM64):# Node-0 amd packagesscp \ downloads-amd/runc.amd64 \ downloads-amd/crictl-v1.32.0-linux-amd64.tar.gz \ downloads-amd/cni-plugins-linux-amd64-v1.6.0.tgz \ downloads-amd/containerd-2.0.0-linux-amd64.tar.gz \ downloads-amd/kubelet \ downloads-amd/kube-proxy \ configs/99-loopback.conf \ configs/containerd-config.toml \ configs/kube-proxy-config.yaml \ units/containerd.service \ units/kubelet.service \ units/kube-proxy.service \ root@node-0:~/Raspberry Pi Node-1 (AMD64):# Node-1 arm packagesscp \ downloads-arm/runc.arm64 \ downloads-arm/crictl-v1.32.0-linux-arm64.tar.gz \ downloads-arm/cni-plugins-linux-arm64-v1.6.0.tgz \ downloads-arm/containerd-2.0.0-linux-arm64.tar.gz \ downloads-arm/kubelet \ downloads-arm/kube-proxy \ configs/99-loopback.conf \ configs/containerd-config.toml \ configs/kube-proxy-config.yaml \ units/containerd.service \ units/kubelet.service \ units/kube-proxy.service \ root@node-1:~/From now on, the commands in this lab must be run on each worker instance: node-0, node-1.SSH into each node to proceed:ssh root@node-0ssh root@node-12.2 Provisioning a Kubernetes Worker Node{ apt update apt -y install socat conntrack ipset kmod # Install kmod for overlay}Check if swap is disabled:No output means swap is disabled. Otherwise, refer to Part 1 to disable swap.2.4 Create the installation directoriesmkdir -p \ /etc/cni/net.d \ /var/lib/kubelet \ /var/lib/kubernetes \ mkdir -p containerd tar -xvf crictl-v1.32.0-linux-amd64.tar.gz tar -xvf containerd-2.0.0-linux-amd64.tar.gz -C containerd tar -xvf cni-plugins-linux-amd64-v1.6.0.tgz -C /opt/cni/bin/ mv runc.amd64 runc chmod +x crictl kubectl kube-proxy kubelet runc mv crictl kubectl kube-proxy kubelet runc /usr/local/bin/ mv containerd/bin/* /bin/}Raspberry Pi Node-1 (AMD64):mkdir -p \ /etc/cni/net.d \ /var/lib/kubelet \ /var/lib/kubernetes \ mkdir -p containerd tar -xvf crictl-v1.32.0-linux-arm64.tar.gz tar -xvf containerd-2.0.0-linux-arm64.tar.gz -C containerd tar -xvf cni-plugins-linux-arm64-v1.6.0.tgz -C /opt/cni/bin/ mv runc.arm64 runc chmod +x crictl kubectl kube-proxy kubelet runc mv crictl kubectl kube-proxy kubelet runc /usr/local/bin/ mv containerd/bin/* /bin/}mv 10-bridge.conf 99-loopback.conf /etc/cni/net.d/{ mkdir -p /etc/containerd/ mv containerd-config.toml /etc/containerd/config.toml mv containerd.service /etc/systemd/system/}{ mv kubelet-config.yaml /var/lib/kubelet/ mv kubelet.service /etc/systemd/system/# Configure the Kubernetes Proxy{ mv kube-proxy-config.yaml /var/lib/kube-proxy/ mv kube-proxy.service /etc/systemd/system/2.6 Start the Worker Servicessystemctl daemon-reloadsystemctl enable containerd kubelet kube-proxysystemctl start containerd kubelet kube-proxysystemctl status containerd kubelet kube-proxyYou may need to wait for a few seconds for the kubelet service to be fully operational.Also check kernal logs for any errors regarding the file system:2.7 Repeat for All Worker NodesFollow the same steps for both node-0 (EC2 A) and node-1(Raspberry Pi).From the jumpbox, check if the worker nodes joined successfully:ssh root@server \ "kubectl get nodes \ --kubeconfig admin.kubeconfig"You should see something like:NAME STATUS ROLES AGE VERSIONnode-0 Ready <none> 1m v1.28.3node-1 Ready <none> 10s v1.28.33. Configuring kubectl for Remote AccessWe’ll set up a local kubeconfig for the user so you can run kubectl commands without specifying --kubeconfig each time.You should be able to ping server.kubernetes.local based on the /etc/hosts DNS entry you configured previously:curl -k --cacert ca.crt \ https://server.kubernetes.local:6443/version{ "major": "1", "gitVersion": "v1.32.0", "gitCommit": "70d3cc986aa8221cd1dfb1121852688902d3bf53", "gitTreeState": "clean", "buildDate": "2024-12-11T17:59:15Z", "goVersion": "go1.23.3", "platform": "linux/arm64"Generate a kubeconfig file suitable for authenticating as the admin user:{ kubectl config set-cluster kubernetes-the-hard-way \ --certificate-authority=ca.crt \ --embed-certs=true \ --server=https://server.kubernetes.local:6443 kubectl config set-credentials admin \ --client-certificate=admin.crt \ kubectl config set-context kubernetes-the-hard-way \ --cluster=kubernetes-the-hard-way \ kubectl config use-context kubernetes-the-hard-way}Check the version of the remote Kubernetes cluster:Client Version: v1.32.0Kustomize Version: v5.5.0List the nodes in the remote Kubernetes cluster:NAME STATUS ROLES AGE VERSIONnode-0 Ready <none> 5m38s v1.32.0node-1 Ready <none> 3m32s v1.32.04. Provisioning Pod Network RoutesWe’ll allow traffic from WireGuard (wg0) to the pod network (cni0) on each worker node. This ensures that pods on node-0 and node-1 can communicate back to the jumpbox or other hosts.From your jumpbox machine, SSH into node-0:iptables -A FORWARD -i wg0 -o cni0 -s 10.0.0.0/16 -d 10.244.1.0/24 -j ACCEPTiptables -A FORWARD -i wg0 -o cni0 -s 10.0.0.0/16 -d 10.244.2.0/24 -j ACCEPTThese commands modify the firewall settings to allow traffic from the WireGuard interface wg0 to the container network interface cni0. This ensures that network packets can pass between the WireGuard network 10.0.0.0/16 and the Kubernetes pod networks on each node - 10.244.1.0/24 or 10.244.2.0/24.We’ll confirm our cluster is functioning by creating a secret, deploying an Nginx app, and verifying logs and network connectivity.From the jumpbox container, ensure the server container has bsdmainutils:ssh root@server 'apt update && apt install -y bsdmainutils'kubectl create secret generic kubernetes-the-hard-way \ --from-literal="mykey=mydata"Print a hexdump of the kubernetes-the-hard-way secret stored in etcd:ssh root@server \'etcdctl get /registry/secrets/default/kubernetes-the-hard-way | hexdump -C'00000000 2f 72 65 67 69 73 74 72 79 2f 73 65 63 72 65 74 |/registry/secret|00000010 73 2f 64 65 66 61 75 6c 74 2f 6b 75 62 65 72 6e |s/default/kubern|00000020 65 74 65 73 2d 74 68 65 2d 68 61 72 64 2d 77 61 |etes-the-hard-wa|00000030 79 0a 6b 38 73 3a 65 6e 63 3a 61 65 73 63 62 63 |y.k8s:enc:aescbc|00000040 3a 76 31 3a 6b 65 79 31 3a 67 0e 3d 21 19 94 e1 |:v1:key1:g.=!...|00000050 56 92 3f ed 27 8a 1e ee 78 4e 3e 61 a5 40 6a 3b |V.?.'...xN>a.@j;|00000060 71 62 0d b2 50 76 69 0c f8 4e d6 b5 5c 24 11 c3 |qb..Pvi..N..\$..|00000070 e5 3c 16 ae 42 5f 00 9e 44 0d cf fd bb b0 e0 14 |.<..B_..D.......|00000080 d6 a0 a9 0d 5c 8d 09 5e 78 5c a8 ae 96 7a ca 42 |....\..^x\...z.B|00000090 19 b3 3e 67 d6 74 3a 1b 4a 35 40 94 36 cc 2b 81 |..>g.t:.J5@.6.+.|000000a0 e6 48 61 ac 2e 78 c6 8b df b7 66 a4 d6 20 cc 88 |.Ha..x....f.. ..|000000b0 25 db 00 6b b5 59 68 e4 6d 55 0f 77 2a 9c 9c bf |%..k.Yh.mU.w*...|000000c0 92 09 d4 45 c7 df 87 1b a8 92 2f af 9a 3c 6b 9f |...E....../..<k.|000000d0 5e 0a 94 32 c3 53 07 37 31 93 3a 0d be 9c dc 05 |^..2.S.71.:.....|000000e0 6b 3b a0 50 f6 9b c5 60 5a 7c 34 b4 52 01 d6 b3 |k;.P...`Z|4.R...|000000f0 f1 7b c9 82 5b ee 71 fc 0b 16 d8 b9 6b 42 53 27 |.{..[.q.....kBS'|00000100 e1 96 d4 e9 6d b4 6e d0 65 75 3f d3 d6 66 4d 24 |....m.n.eu?..fM$|00000110 82 9f dc 3c d3 fb 2c eb 38 4a 9e c7 01 6e bb 69 |...<..,.8J...n.i|00000120 db 27 2e ef 72 0c 71 b5 4f 1b a0 37 64 60 b7 d8 |.'..r.q.O..7d`..|00000130 dc 58 e5 34 57 fa 8d 48 a1 1e 87 43 94 f7 54 42 |.X.4W..H...C..TB|00000140 14 6c 0b 0e 13 b1 81 bc cf 9a ac 3b bd 8d 65 41 |.l.........;..eA|00000150 c6 90 11 46 34 0d 79 c8 e6 0a |...F4.y...|Create an nginx deployment:kubectl create deployment nginx \ --image=nginx:latestList the pods created by the nginx deployment:kubectl get pods -l app=nginxNAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGEnginx-56fcf95486-c8dnx 1/1 Running 0 8sRetrieve the full name of the nginx pod:POD_NAME=$(kubectl get pods -l app=nginx \ -o jsonpath="{.items[0].metadata.name}")Forward port 8080 on your local machine to port 80 of the nginx pod:kubectl port-forward $POD_NAME 8080:80Forwarding from 127.0.0.1:8080 -> 80Forwarding from [::1]:8080 -> 80In a new terminal make an HTTP request using the forwarding address:curl --head http://127.0.0.1:8080You should see a response like this:HTTP/1.1 200 OKServer: nginx/1.25.3Date: Sun, 29 Oct 2023 01:44:32 GMTContent-Type: text/htmlLast-Modified: Tue, 24 Oct 2023 13:46:47 GMTETag: "6537cac7-267"Print the nginx pod logs:...127.0.0.1 - - [01/Nov/2023:06:10:17 +0000] "HEAD / HTTP/1.1" 200 0 "-" "curl/7.88.1" "-"kubectl exec -ti $POD_NAME -- nginx -vnginx version: nginx/1.25.3Expose the nginx deployment using a NodePort service:kubectl create service nodeport nginx --tcp=80:80 --node-port=31703Retrieve the NodePort assigned to the nginx service:NODE_PORT=$(kubectl get svc nginx \ --output=jsonpath='{range .spec.ports[0]}{.nodePort}')Make an HTTP request using the IP address and the nginx NodePort:curl -I http://node-0:${NODE_PORT}HTTP/1.1 200 OKServer: nginx/1.25.3Date: Sun, 29 Oct 2023 05:11:15 GMTContent-Type: text/htmlLast-Modified: Tue, 24 Oct 2023 13:46:47 GMTETag: "6537cac7-267"If you’re done experimenting, remove containers and resources to avoid extra costs or clutter.6.1 Stop & Remove Docker Containerssudo docker stop jumpbox serversudo docker remove jumpbox serversudo docker stop node-0sudo docker remove node-0sudo docker stop node-1sudo docker remove node-1Remove the /mnt directory from the node hosts:6.3 Delete the EC2 Instances (Optional)If you have creates the EC2 A and EC2 B just for the purposes of this guide, terminate them using the AWS Console.Congratulations! You’ve completed your hybrid Kubernetes cluster across AWS EC2 and Raspberry Pi, with: connectivity via WireGuard and kubeconfigs for each cluster component at rest in etcdControl plane and worker nodes deployed manually using an nginx smoke testThis concludes our step-by-step journey through “Kubernetes the Hard Way” with Docker containers, Raspberry Pi, and AWS EC2. We hope this deep dive has:Expanded your understanding of , networking, and security.Demonstrated how to etcd, the control plane, and worker nodes manually.Shown practical ways to and your cluster.Feel free to explore additional features, add-ons, or further enhancements. Happy Kubernetes-ing!

item.title: Docker Interview Questions and Answers
item.author: Harikrishnan M
item.content:encoded: Docker is an essential tool in modern DevOps and cloud computing. Below is a comprehensive list of important Docker interview questions and answers, ranging from basic to advanced, covering concepts, commands, and best practices.Docker is a platform that enables developers to build, package, and deploy applications in containers. It provides lightweight, portable, and consistent environments across different computing environments.2. What are the advantages of using Docker?Portability: Runs consistently across different environments.Efficiency: Uses fewer system resources than virtual machines.Scalability: Easy to scale up or down based on demand.Faster deployment: Speeds up CI/CD pipelines.3. What is a Docker Container?A Docker container is an isolated, lightweight, and portable runtime environment that includes everything needed to run an application (code, runtime, libraries, dependencies, and configurations).4. What is a Docker Image?A Docker image is a read-only template that contains the application and its dependencies. Containers are created from images.# Pull an image from Docker Hubdocker pull nginx# Run a container from the imagedocker run -d --name my_nginx -p 80:80 nginx5. What is the difference between Docker Image and Container?A is a blueprint used to create containers.A is a running instance of an image.Docker Hub is a cloud-based registry for sharing and distributing Docker images.A Dockerfile is a script containing a set of instructions to automate the creation of a Docker image.FROM ubuntu:latestRUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y nginxCOPY index.html /var/www/html/CMD ["nginx", "-g", "daemon off;"]8. Explain the lifecycle of a Docker container.The lifecycle consists of: — Build an image using docker build. — Create and start a container using docker run. — Temporarily stop and resume a running container. — Gracefully stop a container using docker stop. — Forcefully terminate a container using docker kill. — Delete a container using docker rm.9. What is the difference between CMD and ENTRYPOINT in a Dockerfile? sets the default command but can be overridden at runtime. is the main executable and is not easily overridden.CMD ["echo", "Hello World"]ENTRYPOINT ["ping"]10. How do you list running Docker containers?docker ps # Shows active containersdocker ps -a # Shows all containers11. How do you stop and remove a running container?# Stop a containerdocker stop my_container# Remove a containerdocker rm my_container12. What is a Docker Volume?A Docker Volume is a mechanism for persisting data between container restarts.# Create and use a volumedocker volume create my_volumedocker run -d -v my_volume:/data nginx13. What is the difference between a Volume and a Bind Mount? is managed by Docker and stored in /var/lib/docker/volumes/. maps a specific host directory to a container.14. What is Docker Compose?Docker Compose is a tool to define and run multi-container applications.version: '3.8'services: image: nginx - "80:80" image: mysql MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD: root15. What is the difference between Docker Swarm and Kubernetes?: Native clustering and orchestration for Docker.: More powerful, widely adopted container orchestration platform.16. What is a Multi-Stage Build in Docker?A multi-stage build reduces image size by separating the build process into different stages.FROM golang:1.19 AS builderWORKDIR /appRUN go build -o myappFROM alpine:latestWORKDIR /root/COPY --from=builder /app/myapp .CMD ["./myapp"]17. How do you scan Docker images for vulnerabilities?You can use docker scan or tools like Trivy.18. How do you restart a container automatically?docker run -d --restart always nginx19. How do you optimize Docker images?Use lightweight base images (e.g., alpine).Remove unnecessary dependencies.Reduce the number of image layers.20. How do you monitor Docker containers?Use built-in commands and monitoring tools.docker stats # Real-time resource usagedocker logs my_container # View logsMastering these Docker concepts and commands will help you excel in your DevOps interviews. Keep practicing with real-world scenarios and hands-on projects. Happy learning!

item.title: AWS MSK from Scratch: A Beginner’s Guide with Real-Time usecase
item.author: Harikrishnan M
item.content:encoded: AWS Managed Streaming for Apache Kafka (MSK) is a fully managed service that makes it easy to build and run applications using Apache Kafka. Kafka is a popular open-source event streaming platform used for real-time data processing. AWS MSK removes the complexity of setting up and managing Kafka clusters, providing a scalable and reliable streaming solution.In this guide, we’ll walk through AWS MSK from scratch, setting up a real-time streaming pipeline with a practical example. This tutorial is beginner-friendly and designed for those new to Kafka and AWS MSK.Step 1: Understanding Kafka and AWS MSKApache Kafka is a distributed event streaming platform used for: between applicationsReal-time data processingEvent-driven architecture and monitoring (No manual cluster management) (Handles large volumes of data seamlessly) (Integration with AWS IAM and encryption features) (Built-in Amazon CloudWatch integration) (Automated backups and high availability)Step 2: Setting Up AWS MSK ClusterIAM permissions to create MSK clusters, EC2 instances, and security groupsBasic knowledge of AWS services like VPC, EC2, and IAM2.1 Create a VPC and SubnetsAWS MSK requires a VPC with private subnets.2.2 Create an AWS MSK ClusterChoose for full control my-msk-cluster kafka.t3.small (for testing) 3 (minimum recommended for high availability) Select the created VPC Attach the previously created security groupClick and wait for provisioning (~30 min)2.3 Get the MSK Bootstrap ServersOnce the cluster is active:Go to AWS Console > MSK > ClustersSelect the cluster and copy the URLStep 3: Setting Up Kafka Producer & ConsumerTo test our MSK cluster, we will create a (sends messages) and a (receives messages).3.1 Launch an EC2 InstanceLaunch a new EC2 instance with: Allow SSH and Kafka ports (9092) Create or use an existing key pairConnect to the instance via SSH:ssh -i my-key.pem ec2-user@your-ec2-ip3.2 Install Kafka CLI ToolsRun the following commands in the EC2 terminal:sudo yum update -ywget https://archive.apache.org/dist/kafka/3.0.0/kafka_2.13-3.0.0.tgztar -xvzf kafka_2.13-3.0.0.tgzcd kafka_2.13-3.0.03.3 Configure Kafka Connection to AWS MSKEdit the config/client.properties file to include:bootstrap.servers=<your-bootstrap-servers>security.protocol=SSLssl.truststore.location=/etc/pki/ca-trust/extracted/java/cacertsssl.truststore.password=changeitbin/kafka-topics.sh --create --topic my-first-topic --bootstrap-server <your-bootstrap-servers> --partitions 1 --replication-factor 1 --command-config config/client.propertiesCheck if the topic is created:bin/kafka-topics.sh --list --bootstrap-server <your-bootstrap-servers> --command-config config/client.properties3.5 Start a Kafka Producerbin/kafka-console-producer.sh --topic my-first-topic --bootstrap-server <your-bootstrap-servers> --producer.config config/client.propertiesType a message and press to send it.3.6 Start a Kafka ConsumerOpen another SSH session and run:bin/kafka-console-consumer.sh --topic my-first-topic --bootstrap-server <your-bootstrap-servers> --from-beginning --consumer.config config/client.propertiesYou should see the messages sent by the producer!Step 4: Integrating AWS Lambda with AWS MSKAWS Lambda can process Kafka messages in real-time.4.1 Create an IAM Role for LambdaGo to AWS Console > IAM > RolesCreate a new role with AWSLambdaMSKExecutionRole permissions4.2 Create a Lambda FunctionGo to AWS Console > Lambda > Create FunctionSelect KafkaMessageProcessor Use the created IAM RoleAdd the following Python code:import jsondef lambda_handler(event, context): for record in event['records']: for message in event['records'][record]: print("Message received:", message['value']) return { 'body': json.dumps('Processing complete')4.3 Add MSK as an Event SourceGo to Select and enter the topic detailsAWS MSK simplifies Kafka cluster management and allows seamless event-driven processing with AWS Lambda. In this guide, we:Set up an AWS MSK clusterCreated a Kafka producer and consumerConfigured security for Kafka clientsIntegrated AWS Lambda with MSKThis setup is useful for real-time data pipelines, log processing, and event-driven applications. Start exploring AWS MSK today!Experiment with different Kafka topics and partitionsExplore AWS Glue or Kinesis for data transformationsImplement security best practices with IAM and encryption

item.title: AWS Patch Manager
item.author: Harikrishnan M
item.content:encoded: In the AWS DevOps landscape, maintaining the security and compliance of cloud-based environments is crucial. AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager is a fully managed service that automates the process of patching operating systems and applications for Amazon EC2 and on-premises instances. This blog will guide you through the entire process, from understanding the fundamentals to implementing patching in a real-world scenario.What is AWS Patch Manager?AWS Patch Manager is a feature of AWS Systems Manager (SSM) that helps automate the patching of Windows and Linux instances with security and software updates. It allows you to:Define patch baselines to specify which patches should be applied.Schedule patching using Maintenance Windows.Use compliance reports to track patch status.Automate patching workflows with AWS Lambda and CloudWatch Events.Before we start configuring Patch Manager, ensure you have: with appropriate permissions. (Windows/Linux) with Systems Manager Agent (SSM Agent) installed. installed and configured. with necessary permissions to allow AWS Systems Manager to manage instances. defined to logically group instances for targeted patchingStep 1: Attach IAM Role to EC2 InstancesTo allow Patch Manager to interact with your EC2 instances, you need to assign the appropriate IAM role.Creating an IAM Role for SSMNavigate to → → .Choose and select .Attach the AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore policy.Name the role (e.g., SSMManagedEC2) and create it.Attach this role to your EC2 instances.Step 2: Enable AWS Systems ManagerGo to AWS Systems Manager ConsoleNavigate to and verify that your instances appear in the list.If instances are not listed, ensure the SSM Agent is installed and running:sudo amazon-linux-extras enable ssm-agentsudo yum install -y amazon-ssm-agentsudo systemctl start amazon-ssm-agentsudo systemctl enable amazon-ssm-agentFor Windows instances, install the SSM Agent manually or via PowerShell.Step 3: Define a Patch BaselinePatch baselines define which patches are approved for installation on your instances.Navigate to → .Click .Provide a name (e.g., Linux-Security-Patches).Select the (Amazon Linux, Ubuntu, Windows, etc.).Under , chooseAuto approve critical and security updates.Specify the compliance level.Select an approval delay if needed.Step 4: Register Patch GroupsPatch Groups help apply patches selectively to specific instances.Assign a tag to EC2 instances:aws ec2 create-tags --resources i-xxxxxxxxxx --tags Key=PatchGroup,Value=WebServersIn Patch Manager, link the to the tag (WebServers).Under , select and add the group name.Step 5: Create a Maintenance WindowTo schedule patching, configure a .Navigate to → .Click Create maintenance window.Provide a name (e.g., WebServersPatchWindow).Define the schedule (e.g., cron(0 3 ? * SUN *) for every Sunday at 3 AM).Select Allow unassociated targets and create the window.Step 6: Register Targets to Maintenance WindowSelect the and choose .Define targets using the Patch Group tag (WebServers).Step 7: Configure Patch Installation TaskSelect the and choose .Choose and select AWS-RunPatchBaseline.Set the as instances tagged with PatchGroup=WebServers. Reboot if neededStep 8: Manually Trigger Patch Installation (Optional)If you want to manually install patches on demand, use the following command:aws ssm send-command --document-name "AWS-RunPatchBaseline" --targets "Key=tag:PatchGroup,Values=WebServers" --parameters "Operation=Install" --region <your-region>Step 9: Monitoring Patch ComplianceAWS provides compliance reports to track patch status. Go to → → . Run the following command to check compliance:aws ssm list-compliance-summaries Configure AWS CloudWatch logs to capture patching events.Step 10: Automate Patch Reports using AWS LambdaYou can create an AWS Lambda function to send periodic patch compliance reports to an email or Slack channel.Create a new function and select or .Use to fetch compliance reports.Send data to Amazon SNS for notifications.AWS Patch Manager simplifies and automates the patching of EC2 instances, ensuring security and compliance with minimal manual intervention. By following these steps, you can efficiently manage patching across your AWS infrastructure.Start automating your patch management today and keep your infrastructure secure!AWS Patch Manager was originally published in DevOps.dev on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

item.title: 25 Essential Linux Commands for SRE, DevOps, CloudOps & Platform Engineers
item.author: Harikrishnan M
item.content:encoded: Linux is the backbone of modern cloud infrastructure, and mastering key Linux commands is for SREs (Site Reliability Engineers), DevOps, CloudOps, and Platform Engineers.Here’s a list of 25 essential Linux commands with to help you troubleshoot, automate, and manage cloud infrastructure efficiently.These are fundamental commands for checking system details and managing processes.👉 Displays system uptime, load average, and logged-in users.2️⃣ Display System Information👉 Shows the OS name, kernel version, and architecture.👉 Displays disk usage in a human-readable format.👉 Shows free and used memory in MB.5️⃣ Monitor Real-Time System Performance👉 Displays CPU/memory usage, process list, and system load.6️⃣ Monitor Processes Dynamically👉 A better alternative to top, showing CPU/memory usage visually.🔹 User & Permission ManagementManaging users and permissions is crucial in cloud and platform engineering.👉 Shows active user sessions.👉 Displays user login history.sudo useradd -m devopsuser👉 Creates a new user with a home directory.sudo chown user:group filename👉 Changes ownership of a file/directory.1️⃣1️⃣ Modify File Permissions👉 Assigns read/write/execute permissions.Working with files and directories efficiently is key to cloud and infrastructure automation.1️⃣2️⃣ List Files with Details👉 Lists files with sizes, permissions, and hidden files.1️⃣3️⃣ Find a File by Namefind / -name "server.log"👉 Searches for a file in the system.1️⃣4️⃣ Search for a String in Filesgrep "error" /var/log/syslog👉 Searches for “error” in system logs.1️⃣5️⃣ Monitor Log Files in Real Time👉 Streams the last lines of a log file.Managing cloud and Kubernetes workloads requires solid networking skills.1️⃣6️⃣ Check Network Configuration👉 Displays all network interfaces and IPs.1️⃣7️⃣ Check Network Connectivity👉 Tests connectivity to a remote host.1️⃣8️⃣ Trace Route to a Host👉 Shows the network path to a remote server.👉 Displays open ports and associated processes.2️⃣0️⃣ Monitor Network Traffic👉 Displays real-time bandwidth usage.🔹 Process & Service ManagementManaging services efficiently ensures smooth operations.2️⃣1️⃣ Check Running Processes👉 Lists all processes and filters results.2️⃣2️⃣ Kill a Process by ID👉 Forcefully terminates a process.2️⃣3️⃣ Check Active Servicessystemctl list-units --type=service👉 Lists active services in .sudo systemctl restart nginx👉 Restarts a system service.🔹 File Compression & ArchivingManaging logs and backups requires archiving and compression skills.2️⃣5️⃣ Compress a Directorytar -czvf backup.tar.gz /var/log👉 Compresses the /var/log directory into a .tar.gz archive.These 25 essential Linux commands are crucial for SREs, DevOps, CloudOps, and Platform Engineers. Mastering them will make you more efficient in managing cloud workloads, troubleshooting, and automation.🔥 Which Linux commands do you use daily? Drop a comment below! 🚀

item.title: 9 Essential Automation Scripts Every DevOps Engineer Should Know
item.author: Harikrishnan M
item.content:encoded: 9 Essential Automation Scripts Every DevOps Engineers Should KnowMonitoring and logging are essential for system reliability. Whether you’re managing cloud infrastructure, microservices, or CI/CD pipelines, automation scripts help prevent downtime and operational failures. In this blog, I’ll share 9 essential automation scripts that every DevOps engineer should know. These scripts will help you monitor system performance, detect anomalies, and ensure smooth application functioning.This guide is useful for both beginners and experienced DevOps professionals. Let’s explore these scripts, their implementation, and real-world scenarios where they provide value.1. Disk Space Monitoring Script Running out of disk space can lead to application crashes and failed deployments. A Bash script checks disk usage and sends an alert if it exceeds a threshold.#!/bin/bashTHRESHOLD=85df -h | awk '{if($5+0 > '$THRESHOLD') print $0}' A logging service storing large volumes of data experiences slow performance due to insufficient disk space. This script provides early warnings so that additional storage can be provisioned before reaching critical limits.2. CPU Usage Monitoring Script High CPU usage can indicate an unoptimized application or excessive resource consumption. A Python script monitors CPU usage and sends alerts when it crosses a defined threshold.import psutilif psutil.cpu_percent(interval=1) > 85: print("High CPU Usage Alert!") A machine learning inference server experiences high CPU usage due to multiple simultaneous requests. This script helps identify resource spikes, allowing auto-scaling strategies to be implemented.3. Log Rotation Automation Script Large logs can consume disk space and make debugging difficult. A Bash script compresses and archives old logs, keeping only recent ones.#!/bin/bashLOG_DIR="/var/log/app/"tar -czf $LOG_DIR/archive_$(date +%F).tar.gz $LOG_DIR/*.logrm $LOG_DIR/*.log A video processing application generates massive logs. This script ensures that logs are archived periodically, preventing performance degradation due to excessive file accumulation.4. Service Uptime Monitoring Script Immediate detection of service downtime minimizes disruption. A Python script pings a service endpoint and alerts if it’s unreachable.import requeststry: response = requests.get("http://api.your-service.com") if response.status_code != 200: print("Service Down Alert!")except Exception: print("Service Unreachable!") A real-time stock trading platform depends on API services. This script alerts engineers if an API is unresponsive, allowing them to take immediate action.5. Memory Usage Monitoring Script Low memory availability can cause system crashes. A Bash script checks memory usage and alerts if it falls below a threshold.#!/bin/bashTHRESHOLD=200 # in MBAVAILABLE=$(free -m | awk '/Mem/ {print $7}')if [ $AVAILABLE -lt $THRESHOLD ]; thenfi A containerized AI inference engine occasionally runs out of memory due to unpredictable request patterns. This script provides early warnings to allocate additional resources or restart services before failures occur.6. Docker Container Health Check Script Monitoring container health ensures the stability of microservices. A Bash script checks the health status of running Docker containers.#!/bin/bashfor container in $(docker ps --format "{{.Names}}"); do STATUS=$(docker inspect --format '{{.State.Health.Status}}' $container) echo "Container: $container - Status: $STATUS" An e-commerce backend runs multiple microservices in Docker. This script quickly identifies failing containers, allowing prompt intervention.7. Kubernetes Pod Monitoring Script Pods are the smallest deployable units in Kubernetes, and their health determines application stability. This script checks for non-running pods.#!/bin/bashkubectl get pods --all-namespaces | grep -v 'Running' A ride-sharing app experiences pod failures due to node resource exhaustion. This script identifies the failing pods, enabling immediate troubleshooting.8. Website Availability Check Script Websites going offline can lead to business loss. A Python script checks if a website is up and alerts if it’s down.import requeststry: response = requests.get("http://yourwebsite.com") print("Website is up!") if response.status_code == 200 else print("Website is down!") print("Website Unreachable!") A news platform ensures its main website remains accessible during peak hours using this script.9. Automated Backup Verification Script Backups must be verified to ensure data safety. A Bash script verifies backup integrity.#!/bin/bashBACKUP_DIR="/backups"for file in $BACKUP_DIR/*.tar.gz; do if ! tar -tzf $file > /dev/null; then echo "Corrupted backup file: $file" fi A financial firm automates backup verification to prevent data loss in case of system failures.These scripts automate essential DevOps tasks, improving system reliability and reducing manual efforts. By integrating them into your infrastructure, you can proactively monitor and manage your cloud and on-premise environments.Have you implemented automation scripts in your workflow? Share your experiences and insights in the comments below!
